project: Cámara Libre — H.264 Pipeline Rewrite Blueprint

problem_diagnosis.symptom: Stuck at ~15 FPS regardless of resolution or bitrate
problem_diagnosis.root_cause: 4 memory copies per frame + Dart GC pressure + MethodChannel JNI overhead

current_pipeline_bottlenecks.action: ImageReader YUV420_888 allocates native heap buffer
JNI copies YUV planes into Dart Uint8List (1.3MB @ 720p)
MethodChannel.pushFrame() copies Dart bytes back to Java/Kotlin
onFrameEncoded copies H264 NAL units from native to Dart again

problem_diagnosis.why_15fps: At 30fps, this pipeline moves ~40MB/s of memory bidirectionally. GC pauses + JNI marshalling + _encodingBusy flag dropping frames = hard ceiling at 15fps.

target_architecture.principle: Pixels must NEVER touch Dart heap during streaming. Dart handles UI and socket only.
ideal_pipeline: Hardware H.264 encoder (silicon) → Kotlin ByteBuffer output
Kotlin TCP socket thread → sends NAL units directly to PC
Dart UI thread → start/stop commands + stats display only

implementation_steps.title: Delete YUV MethodChannel traffic
implementation_steps.action: Remove pushFrame() and onFrameEncoded() from MethodChannel. The channel now only carries control commands: start, stop, setBitrate.

implementation_steps.title: Rewrite Kotlin native plugin
implementation_steps.action: Create a single Kotlin class that owns CameraDevice + MediaCodec + Socket. No Flutter camera plugin involved in streaming.

implementation_steps.title: Use Surface-to-Surface zero copy
implementation_steps.action: MediaCodec configured with COLOR_FormatSurface. Camera2 target is encoderSurface directly. No ByteBuffer, no ImageReader.

implementation_steps.title: Move socket to Kotlin
implementation_steps.action: The TCP client lives in Kotlin, not Dart. It reads NAL units from MediaCodec output and writes to socket in a dedicated HandlerThread.

implementation_steps.title: Optional: Preview surface
implementation_steps.action: If you need phone screen preview, add a TextureView Surface as SECOND target in createCaptureSession. WARNING: some devices drop FPS with dual surfaces. Test thoroughly.
implementation_steps.alternative: Skip phone preview; show a low-fps decoded thumbnail from the PC feedback loop, or use a static overlay UI.

implementation_steps.title: Dart UI refactor
implementation_steps.action: Dart only calls method channel for: start(ip, port, width, height, fps, bitrate), stop(), getStats(). No frame data ever crosses the channel.

kotlin_skeleton.description: Core engine. Owns Camera2 lifecycle, MediaCodec, and TCP socket thread.

method_channel_contract.events: None. All frame data stays in Kotlin.

dart_ui_skeleton.description: Dart only orchestrates. No pixel handling.

critical_warnings: NEVER pass YUV planes through MethodChannel. It is architecturally impossible to reach 30fps this way.
COLOR_FormatSurface is the ONLY viable color format for 30fps+ zero-copy encoding.
TEMPLATE_RECORD + fixed [30,30] fps range is mandatory. TEMPLATE_PREVIEW will drop to 15fps under AE control.
If adding a preview TextureView as second surface, test on low-end devices (Samsung A-series, Xiaomi Redmi). Many limit dual-surface throughput.
The socket write in drainEncoder() must be non-blocking or on a separate thread. Do NOT block the MediaCodec output thread.
Use length-prefixed framing (4-byte header + NAL payload) so your PC server parser stays compatible.

competitive_context.droidcam_architecture: Native Android Camera2 + MediaCodec Surface + direct socket. No Flutter/Dart in the pixel path.
competitive_context.your_current_architecture: Flutter camera plugin (ImageReader) → Dart YUV → MethodChannel → Kotlin MediaCodec. Dart owns pixels.
competitive_context.why_this_matters: DroidCam wins on FPS because they never cross the JNI/Dart boundary with uncompressed frames. You must match this architecture to compete.
